items.title: Capture the primary screen with GDI BitBlt on .NET 10 Windows
items.description: On .NET 10 / Windows, capture the primary display by creating a memory device context compatible with the screen DC, selecting a bitmap sized to the primary monitor bounds, and BitBlt-ing the screen DC into it. Graphics.CopyFromScreen wraps this for managed code. Capture only the primary monitor rectangle.
items.applicability: The real capture implementation behind the screen-source abstraction.
items.example: var bmp = new Bitmap(w, h); using var gfx = Graphics.FromImage(bmp); gfx.CopyFromScreen(0, 0, 0, 0, new Size(w, h)); captures the primary screen into a bitmap for encoding.
items.limitation: GDI BitBlt is simple and dependency-free but does not capture hardware-overlay or some DRM-protected surfaces, which appear black; it is also CPU-bound and lower-throughput than DXGI Desktop Duplication.

items.title: Define an IScreenSource abstraction over capture
items.description: Hide the capture mechanism behind an interface (e.g. IScreenSource with a GetFrame/CaptureAsync method returning frame bytes plus metadata). Code that encodes and streams depends on the interface, not on GDI. This lets a deterministic fake replace real capture for tests.
items.applicability: Architecture seam between capture and the streaming/encoding pipeline.
items.example: interface IScreenSource { ValueTask<ScreenFrame> CaptureAsync(CancellationToken ct); int Width { get; } int Height { get; } } with two implementations selected by configuration.
items.limitation: An abstraction adds a small indirection cost and does not by itself guarantee the fake and real sources behave identically; their frame metadata contracts must be tested to stay aligned.

items.title: Provide a deterministic fake screen source for automated tests
items.description: The fake IScreenSource generates frames procedurally (e.g. a moving block, a frame counter painted into the image, or a fixed gradient) so tests are reproducible without a real desktop, display, or session. It must require no GUI and run in CI headlessly.
items.applicability: Automated tests of encoding, framing, and streaming that must run without a desktop.
items.example: FakeScreenSource paints the current frame number into a 640x360 bitmap and increments a counter, so a test can assert the decoded frame number equals the expected sequence.
items.limitation: A fake cannot reveal real-world capture issues (black DRM surfaces, multi-monitor DPI, GPU overlays); it validates the pipeline, not the GDI capture itself, which needs a manual run.

items.title: Use a real capture source only for manual runs
items.description: Real GDI capture requires an interactive desktop session and must be selected explicitly (config flag) for manual demonstration, never as the default in automated test contexts. CI and unit tests use the fake; humans use the real source.
items.applicability: Source selection in dependency injection and test configuration.
items.example: Configuration ScreenSource=Fake is the test/CI default; ScreenSource=Real is set only when an operator runs the client interactively on a Windows desktop.
items.limitation: If a CI agent ever runs in an interactive session, a misconfiguration could select real capture and produce flaky or blank frames; the default must fail safe to fake.

items.title: Encode frames as JPEG with configurable quality
items.description: Encode each captured bitmap to JPEG before transmission. JPEG gives a good size/quality trade-off for screen content at moderate cost. Expose a quality setting (e.g. 1-100) so the operator can trade bandwidth for clarity.
items.applicability: Frame-encoding stage of the streaming pipeline.
items.example: An ImageCodecInfo JPEG encoder with an EncoderParameter Quality=70 compresses a 1080p frame to tens of kilobytes suitable for streaming over a modest link.
items.limitation: JPEG is lossy and blurs sharp text edges at low quality; for crisp text a higher quality or a different codec (PNG/WebP) trades bandwidth, which this simple pipeline does not auto-tune.

items.title: Make frame rate (FPS) configurable
items.description: The target capture/send rate must be a configurable cap (e.g. 1-30 FPS), not hard-coded. Lower FPS reduces CPU and bandwidth; the cap bounds how often the capture loop runs.
items.example: With TargetFps=10 the loop aims for one frame every 100 ms; the operator lowers it to 5 on a slow link.
items.limitation: A high configured FPS cannot be met if capture+encode exceeds the frame budget; the actual rate is min(configured, achievable), so the cap is an upper bound, not a guarantee.

items.title: Attach metadata to every frame
items.description: Each frame must carry metadata: session id, monotonically increasing frame number, width, height, and a capture timestamp. Metadata lets the viewer order frames, drop stale ones, measure latency, and bind frames to a session.
items.example: ScreenFrame { Guid SessionId; long FrameNumber; int Width; int Height; long TimestampUnixMs; byte[] Jpeg } is serialized ahead of the JPEG payload.
items.limitation: Timestamps from the capturing machine are only as accurate as its clock; cross-machine latency derived from them assumes roughly synchronised clocks or measures relative trends rather than absolute one-way delay.

items.title: Client connects outbound; server validates token on connect
items.description: The sharing client initiates an OUTBOUND WebSocket connection to the server/viewer endpoint. On accept, the server first validates the session token from the handshake before any frames flow. Outbound-from-client connection simplifies NAT traversal on the sharer side.
items.applicability: Connection topology and the authentication handshake.
items.example: The client opens ws(s)://server:port/stream?token=...; the server reads the token, validates it, and only then transitions the connection to streaming state.
items.limitation: Outbound-from-client still requires the SERVER to be reachable; if the server is behind NAT without a public address or port-forward, the client cannot reach it (see the packaging/network pack).

items.title: Define an explicit handshake before streaming
items.description: After the socket opens and the token validates, exchange a short handshake: the client announces session id and frame dimensions/format; the server acknowledges and signals 'ready'. Frames start only after the acknowledged handshake.
items.applicability: Protocol state machine at session start.
items.example: Client sends Hello{ sessionId, width, height, format:'jpeg', fps }; server replies Ready{ accepted:true }; the client then begins sending Frame messages.
items.limitation: A rigid handshake makes version mismatches fail fast but requires both ends to agree on the handshake schema; an out-of-date viewer rejects a newer client rather than degrading gracefully unless versioning is built in.

items.title: Define distinct message types: handshake, frame, heartbeat, disconnect
items.description: The protocol uses a small set of typed messages: Hello/Ready (handshake), Frame (metadata + JPEG), Heartbeat (keep-alive), and Disconnect (clean shutdown). A type tag at the start of each message lets the receiver dispatch correctly.
items.applicability: Message framing and dispatch on both ends.
items.example: Each message begins with a one-byte type code: 0x01 Hello, 0x02 Ready, 0x03 Frame, 0x04 Heartbeat, 0x05 Disconnect; the reader switches on the code.
items.limitation: A minimal type set keeps the protocol simple but offers no room for negotiated extensions; adding a capability later needs a new type code and version handling.

items.title: Send periodic heartbeats to detect dead connections
items.description: Both ends exchange heartbeat messages on an interval when no frames are flowing, so a silently dropped TCP connection is detected within a bounded time rather than hanging. A missed-heartbeat threshold triggers teardown.
items.applicability: Keep-alive and liveness detection in the protocol.
items.example: If no frame or heartbeat arrives within 3x the heartbeat interval, the receiver treats the peer as gone, closes the socket, and updates status.
items.limitation: WebSocket already has ping/pong frames; an application-level heartbeat duplicates that but gives explicit control and a clear timeout value the team owns. Tune the threshold to the FPS so low-FPS streams are not falsely dropped.

items.title: Send an explicit disconnect message on clean shutdown
items.description: When the user presses Disconnect or the session ends normally, the client sends a Disconnect message before closing the socket, so the viewer can distinguish an intentional stop from a network failure and update its UI accordingly.
items.example: On Disconnect the client sends Disconnect{ reason:'user' }, awaits the socket close handshake briefly, then closes; the viewer shows 'Sharing ended by user'.
items.limitation: If the network drops at the same moment, the disconnect message may never arrive and the viewer falls back to the heartbeat-timeout path; the explicit message is best-effort, not guaranteed.

items.title: Enforce a connection/stream timeout
items.description: Apply timeouts: a handshake timeout (token + Hello must complete quickly) and a stream-idle timeout (no frame/heartbeat for N seconds closes the session). Timeouts prevent half-open sockets and abandoned sessions from lingering.
items.example: If the token+handshake does not complete within 10 seconds, the server closes the connection; if no frame or heartbeat arrives for 15 seconds mid-session, it closes and frees the session.
items.limitation: Aggressive timeouts can drop legitimate but slow links; the values must be tuned to expected network conditions and the configured FPS to avoid false disconnects.

items.title: Drop stale frames; keep only the latest
items.description: Screen sharing is latency-sensitive and tolerant of loss: if the encoder/network falls behind, discard older queued frames and send the newest. A single-slot 'latest frame wins' buffer prevents a growing backlog and rubber-banding.
items.applicability: Send-side queue policy between capture and socket.
items.example: The capture loop writes the latest frame into a one-slot buffer; if the sender is busy when a new frame arrives, the previous unsent frame is overwritten rather than queued.
items.limitation: Dropping frames is correct for live viewing but unsuitable if a complete frame history were ever required (it is not, here); the viewer sees current state, not every intermediate frame.

items.title: Maintain a monotonic frame counter
items.description: Each session increments a frame counter starting at zero or one. The counter must be monotonic per session and is carried in frame metadata, enabling the viewer to detect drops/reordering and tests to assert that streaming progresses.
items.applicability: Frame-numbering in the capture loop and tests.
items.example: The capture loop assigns frame.FrameNumber = ++_counter; a loopback streaming test asserts the received counter strictly increases across a burst of frames.
items.limitation: After a frame drop the counter has gaps by design; the viewer must treat gaps as expected loss, not corruption, and the counter resets per session so it is not unique across sessions.

items.title: Implement adaptive FPS under load
items.description: When encode or send time consistently exceeds the frame budget, automatically reduce the effective FPS toward a floor; when headroom returns, raise it back toward the configured cap. Adaptation keeps latency bounded instead of building a backlog.
items.applicability: Runtime rate-control in the capture loop.
items.example: If the moving average of encode+send time exceeds the current frame interval for several frames, halve the rate down to a 2 FPS floor; recover by stepping the rate up when timings improve.
items.limitation: Adaptation logic adds complexity and can oscillate if thresholds are poorly chosen; it must use hysteresis (different up/down thresholds) and stay within the operator's configured cap and floor.

items.title: Measure latency and last-frame time
items.description: Track and expose per-session metrics: approximate end-to-end latency (from frame timestamp to viewer receipt) and the time since the last frame. These feed the dashboard/health view and let stale sessions be spotted.
items.applicability: Telemetry surfaced to the dashboard and health checks.
items.example: The viewer computes latency as receiptTime - frame.TimestampUnixMs and records lastFrameAt; the dashboard shows 'latency ~120 ms, last frame 0.2 s ago'.
items.limitation: Latency derived from cross-machine timestamps is only approximate without clock synchronisation; treat it as a trend indicator, and on a healthy LAN it is more reliable than over a variable internet path.
